course_aims:
In this course, students will be introduced to a variety of algorithm design techniques (greedy, dynamic programming, divide and conquer, etc), and most importantly learn how to apply them in different settings.

This course aims to develop students' theoretical knowledge in order to design correct and efficient software, as well as problem solving, critical thinking and written communication skills.

By understanding algorithm design principles, analysing and evaluating algorithms, and applying these principles to solve unfamiliar problems, students will become more capable and responsible problem solvers.
attendance_requirements: Students are strongly encouraged to attend all classes and review lecture recordings.

assessments.description:
Portfolio consists of responses to a collection of formative tasks completed during the term.

Tasks will be made available upon the commencement of each module. Individual written or oral feedback will be provided promptly upon submission (in labs or online), after which the student can resubmit until the task is complete or the deadline expires.

This assessment uses contract grading. Students are expected to complete tasks up to and including their chosen grade target (which can be changed at any time), with more open-ended and extension tasks to distinguish between the highest achievers. The final portfolio will be evaluated using delayed summative assessment, with input from the student's tutor and a formula to quantify tasks completed.

Overall the portfolio tasks should take around 6-8 hours per week.
learning_outcomes: CLO1 : Explain how standard design techniques are used to develop algorithms.
CLO2 : Solve problems by creatively applying algorithm design techniques.
CLO3 : Communicate algorithmic ideas at different levels of abstraction.
CLO4 : Evaluate the efficiency of algorithms and justify their correctness.
CLO5 : Apply the LaTeX typesetting system to produce high-quality technical documents.

assessments.description:
The final examination is a 2 hour in person invigilated exam that tests critical thinking and general understanding of the course material, in addition to the application of algorithm design techniques to analyse algorithms and solve unseen problems.

Marking will be completed using a rubric. There is a hurdle of 40% on the final exam.
assessments.hurdles: Students receiving less than 40% of available marks will receive a UF grade.
